medications.indication_rationale: First-line for acute urticaria is a non-sedating H1 antihistamine, up-dosed when the standard dose fails (as here with loratadine). The source clinician gave cetirizine 20 mg (2x standard) for the acute flare; guidelines support up-dosing to as much as 4x standard.
medications.dose_route_frequency: cetirizine 20 mg orally once as an acute dose, then a regular daily up-dosed regimen as directed

medications.indication_rationale: An alternative non-sedating H1 antihistamine for ongoing symptomatic control; the source clinician prescribed fexofenadine 180 mg up to twice daily (off-label up-dosing) for acute symptomatic control.
medications.dose_route_frequency: fexofenadine 180 mg orally, up to twice daily (off-label up-dosing per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: A short course of oral corticosteroid can be used to settle a severe acute urticarial flare that is highly distressing and antihistamine-refractory. The source clinician prescribed prednisolone 25 mg daily for 3 days.
medications.dose_route_frequency: prednisolone 25 mg orally once daily for 3 days
medications.deprescribing_note: Short fixed course — stop after 3 days without taper; do not continue as maintenance for acute urticaria.

medications.indication_rationale: Adrenaline is the treatment for ANAPHYLAXIS, which this patient does not have — skin-limited urticaria with no airway, respiratory or circulatory involvement. Reaching for adrenaline here is an error of commission driven by over-calling anaphylaxis. (It becomes indicated only if the safety-net airway/anaphylaxis features develop, per node 13.)

medications.drug_name: sedating (first-generation) antihistamine, e.g. promethazine, as first-line
medications.indication_rationale: Sedating antihistamines are not recommended first-line for urticaria (sedation, anticholinergic and psychomotor effects); a non-sedating agent up-dosed is preferred. A sedating agent at night is at most a limited adjunct if itch prevents sleep.
There is no bacterial infection; urticaria is not an indication for antibiotics. Prescribing them is an error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the non-sedating antihistamine on a regular fixed schedule rather than only when the itch becomes severe, for the duration of the flare.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Pre-emptive, regular H1 blockade gives better symptom control than waiting for breakthrough itching, and reduces the counterproductive scratch-itch cycle.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply cool compresses to itchy areas and avoid hot showers, tight clothing, and vigorous exertion.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Heat, friction and exertion are physical triggers that predictably worsen urticaria; cooling settles the itch.

patient_education_points.point: This is a hives reaction. Each individual welt should fade within 24 hours without leaving a mark, but new crops can keep appearing for several days to a few weeks before it settles.

Non-sedating antihistamines block the chemical (histamine) causing the welts and itch; because a standard dose did not help, a higher regular dose is used, and a short steroid course can calm a severe flare.

Seek emergency help immediately (call 000) if you develop swelling of the lips, tongue or throat, a change in your voice, difficulty breathing or swallowing, or feel faint — these are warning signs of a more serious allergic reaction.
Apply cool compresses and avoid hot showers, tight clothing and intense exercise, which make hives worse.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: resolution of the rash, whether new crops are still erupting, response to up-dosed antihistamines, and the need for allergy referral only if it becomes chronic (>6 weeks) or recurrent
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any airway swelling, voice change, breathing/swallowing difficulty or faintness (call 000 immediately); or individual weals lasting >24 hours, becoming painful, or leaving bruising (prompt GP review)

must_include_items: Recognise the presentation as benign, self-limiting acute urticaria (not anaphylaxis) after actively screening for airway/respiratory/circulatory red flags
Advise up-dosing a non-sedating H1 antihistamine because a standard dose has already failed (not simply repeating the same dose)
Give explicit, specific airway/anaphylaxis safety-netting with a 000 threshold (lip/tongue/throat swelling, voice change, breathing/swallowing difficulty, faintness)
Give symptomatic advice (cool compresses; avoid heat, tight clothing, exertion)
Arrange GP follow-up in a few days and reassure about the typically self-limiting course
acceptable_alternatives: A short course of oral corticosteroid (e.g. prednisolone) for a severe, distressing, antihistamine-refractory flare
Switching to or adding a different non-sedating antihistamine (e.g. fexofenadine)
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for and safety-net airway/anaphylaxis features
Failing to recommend any antihistamine, or leaving the patient on an already-failed standard dose without up-dosing advice
errors_of_commission: Treating the presentation as anaphylaxis and recommending adrenaline / an autoinjector when there are no airway/respiratory/circulatory features
Over-triaging a haemodynamically stable, skin-limited patient to ED or a 000 ambulance
Ordering allergy testing, serum tryptase, or routine bloods in a first acute episode
Recommending a sedating antihistamine as first-line, or prescribing antibiotics
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES correct low-acuity triage (telehealth/GP-manageable) TOGETHER WITH explicit airway/anaphylaxis safety-netting. Omitting the safety-net is a serious error even if the diagnosis is right; over-treating as anaphylaxis or over-triaging to emergency care without red flags is a defining error of commission.
